1: Then YHWH said to Moses, 'Go in to Pharaoh and say to him, Thus says YHWH, Let my people go, that they may serve me. But if you refuse to let them go, behold, I will plague all your country with frogs (tz'phardea):' Tz'phardea = frog (Egyptian: qrr or krr; the frog was sacred in Egypt as a symbol of fertility and new life). Heqet was the Egyptian frog-headed goddess of childbirth and fertility — the patron deity of the midwives who assisted at birth. The frog plague inverted Heqet's domain: instead of frogs symbolizing the blessing of new life, they became an overwhelming infestation. The midwives (meyaldot) who defied Pharaoh's infanticide command in Exodus 1:15-21 may have invoked Heqet's protection; now Heqet's symbol becomes Egypt's plague.
2: The Nile shall swarm (sharatz) with frogs that shall come up into your house and into your bedroom and on your bed: Sharatz = to swarm/teem (the same verb as the swarming of sea creatures in Genesis 1:20 and the swarming of life in the aftermath of creation). The frog infestation reverses the creation blessing of abundance into a curse of overwhelming proliferation. The specific locations — house, bedroom, bed — represent the private, intimate, and restful spaces of life; the plague penetrates to the innermost domestic spaces that Egyptian architecture was designed to protect.
3: The frogs shall come up on you and on your people and on all your servants: The comprehensive social coverage — Pharaoh, his people, all his servants — mirrors the Nile's comprehensive coverage in the first plague. Every social stratum of Egyptian society is affected equally. The equality of the plague's impact across all social classes is itself a theological statement: YHWH's judgment does not respect the social hierarchy Egypt has constructed.
4: Then Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh and said, 'Thus says YHWH, Let my people go that they may serve me': The repeated demand formula (shalach et-ami v'ya'avduni = let my people go that they may serve me) appears in seven of the ten plague cycles. The consistency of the demand throughout the plague narrative demonstrates that the purpose is not punishment but release: each plague is an opportunity for Pharaoh to comply with the original request.
5: And YHWH said to Moses, 'Say to Aaron, Stretch out your hand with your staff over the rivers, the canals, and the ponds, and make frogs come up on the land of Egypt': The comprehensive water system of Egypt (rivers/Nile channels, canals, ponds/marshes) is again the source of the plague — the same waters that turned to blood now produce frogs. The sequential use of the same water system for two different plagues establishes the Nile as the primary locus of divine action: Egypt's greatest blessing is YHWH's instrument of judgment.
6: So Aaron stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt, and the frogs came up and covered the land of Egypt: 'Covered the land' (v'takh's ha-tz'pharde'a et-eretz Mitzrayim) uses the same 'cover' (khasa) language as the locust plague (Exodus 10:15) and the Red Sea covering the Egyptians (Exodus 14:28). The covering pattern establishes each plague as progressively more total in its coverage.
7: But the magicians did the same by their secret arts and made frogs come up on the land of Egypt: Again the chartumim replicate the plague, making the situation worse rather than better. The replication of the frog plague is their final successful imitation; they will fail at the lice plague and be physically incapacitated by the boils. The trajectory of the magicians — capable of replication → unable to replicate → physically defeated — charts Egypt's progressive defeat.
8: Then Pharaoh called Moses and Aaron and said, 'Plead with YHWH to take away the frogs from me and from my people, and I will let the people go to sacrifice to YHWH': Pharaoh's first prayer request to YHWH through Moses — and his first offer to comply, conditional on relief. This is a significant theological moment: the god-king of Egypt who declared 'Who is YHWH?' (Exodus 5:2) is now asking YHWH's servants to intercede for him. The frogs in Pharaoh's bed may have been more personally intolerable than the blood in the Nile — the plague penetrated his personal space in a way the water contamination did not.
9: Moses said to Pharaoh, 'Be pleased to command me when I am to plead for you and for your servants and for your people, that the frogs be cut off from you and your houses and be left only in the Nile.' Moses's offer to let Pharaoh set the timing of the plague's removal is a diplomatic and theological stratagem — if Moses names the time himself, Pharaoh could claim the cessation was coincidental; if Pharaoh names the time and the frogs stop at exactly that moment, the divine control of the timing is demonstrated. The demonstration of precise divine control over timing is itself a refutation of natural explanations.
10: 'Tomorrow,' said Pharaoh. And Moses said, 'Be it as you say, so that you may know that there is no one like YHWH our God.' Pharaoh's choice of 'tomorrow' (machar) rather than 'immediately' has puzzled commentators. Possibly: he is buying one more night of comfort (the frogs being marginally preferable to another night of compliance)? Or he does not fully believe Moses can deliver? Or he is testing whether Moses has real control? The result — frogs removed precisely at the time Pharaoh named — proves divine precision.
11: The frogs shall go away from you and your houses and your servants and your people. They shall be left only in the Nile. The precision of the divine response — frogs removed except from the Nile — matches the precision of the divine promise. The Nile (the source and proper domain of frogs in Egypt's ecology) retains its frogs; the unnatural infestation is removed, but the natural order is restored. The restoration of normalcy is itself a sign of divine control.
12: So Moses and Aaron went out from Pharaoh, and Moses cried to YHWH about the frogs, as he had agreed with Pharaoh: Moses's intercessory prayer (vayyitz'aq Moshe el-YHWH) on behalf of Pharaoh is theologically striking — the prophet of YHWH prays for the relief of YHWH's enemy. The pattern of intercession on behalf of Pharaoh (Exodus 8:12, Exodus 8:28-30, Exodus 9:33, Exodus 10:18) establishes Moses as a mediator figure who stands between YHWH's justice and human need, even the need of those who oppress his people.
13: And YHWH did according to the word of Moses. The frogs died out in the houses, the courtyards, and the fields. The three spatial zones — houses (domestic interior), courtyards (semi-domestic transition space), fields (agricultural exterior) — mark the complete clearing of the plague from the inhabited and agricultural landscape. The precision of the fulfillment (exactly as Pharaoh requested, exactly at the time he named) demonstrates YHWH's sovereign command of natural processes.
14: And they gathered them together in heaps, and the land stank: The decomposing frog carcasses after the plague ended would have created a secondary public health crisis — rotting amphibians in the Egyptian summer heat. The stench (tivash ha'aretz = the land reeked) of the aftermath mirrors the stench of the Nile during the blood plague (Exodus 7:18,21). The aftermath of each plague is as unpleasant as the plague itself, accumulating pressure on Pharaoh to comply.
15: But when Pharaoh saw that there was a respite, he hardened his heart and would not listen to them, as YHWH had said: The relief-hardening pattern is established here explicitly: Pharaoh's behavioral change was driven entirely by immediate distress, not genuine recognition of YHWH's authority. The psychology of hardening under pressure followed by return to the original position when pressure is removed is a consistent feature of human response to divine discipline that the prophets later address (Isaiah 26:9-10; Hosea 5:15-6:4).
16: Then YHWH said to Moses, 'Say to Aaron, Stretch out your staff and strike the dust of the earth, so that it may become gnats (kinnim) throughout all the land of Egypt.' Kinnim = the third plague; the exact identity of the insect is debated — the Hebrew kinnim appears only in the plague narrative and Psalms 105:31; the LXX reads 'gnats' (skniphes); the Vulgate reads 'flies' (sciniphes); the Talmud (Sanhedrin 67b) interprets as lice; the word may be related to the Egyptian word 'snn' for a type of mosquito or sand fly. The distinctive feature of this plague is its origin from the dust (afar ha'aretz) rather than the Nile.
17: They did so. Aaron stretched out his hand with his staff and struck the dust of the earth, and there were gnats on man and beast. All the dust of the earth became gnats in all the land of Egypt: The metamorphosis of the dust itself (kol afar ha'aretz hayah kinnim) — not just gnats coming from dust but the dust becoming insects — exceeds anything in the Egyptian magical tradition. The dust-to-insect transformation inverts the creation narrative: in Genesis 2:7 YHWH formed Adam from dust (afar) and breathed life into him; here the dust of Egypt produces a pestilential swarm.
18: The magicians tried by their secret arts to produce gnats, but they could not. So there were gnats on man and beast: The chartumim's failure at the lice/gnats plague is the pivotal moment in the Egyptian magicians' story. They have been matching Moses and Aaron plague-for-plague (unsuccessfully, since they can only add to the plagues, not remove them); now they cannot even replicate. Their failure signals a qualitative difference between YHWH's power and Egyptian magic. This is the last plague from which the chartumim attempt replication.
19: And the magicians said to Pharaoh, 'This is the finger of God (etzba Elohim).' But Pharaoh's heart was hardened, and he would not listen to them, as YHWH had said: 'Finger of God' (etzba Elohim) — the Egyptian priests' own confession. The term 'finger of God' appears also in Exodus 31:18 (the stone tablets written by YHWH's own finger) and Deuteronomy 9:10. In Egyptian religious texts, various deities were described as acting through their 'finger' (dba = finger in Egyptian) in magical contexts; the chartumim are using their own theological framework to acknowledge defeat. Notably, they say 'Elohim,' not YHWH — they acknowledge a superior divine power without necessarily identifying it as the Hebrew God by name. Pharaoh dismisses even the testimony of his own elite priests.
20: Then YHWH said to Moses, 'Rise up early in the morning and present yourself to Pharaoh, as he goes out to the water, and say to him, Thus says YHWH, Let my people go that they may serve me.' The 'going out to the water' venue recurs (cf. Exodus 7:15) — the Nile confrontation is the deliberate setting for the covenant demand, making the declaration of YHWH's sovereign authority at the very site of Egypt's primary deity (the Nile/Hapy). The early morning (hashkem baboqer) intercept catches Pharaoh before his daily business begins, maximizing the disruptive impact of the confrontation.
21: 'For if you will not let my people go, behold, I will send swarms of flies (arov) on you and your servants and your people, and into your houses. The houses of the Egyptians shall be full of swarms of flies, and also the ground on which they stand.' Arov = the fourth plague's identification is disputed. The LXX reads 'dog-fly' (kunomuia = a particularly vicious biting fly, possibly the Stomoxys calcitrans or a tabanid fly). Others read 'swarms' generally or 'mixture of wild animals.' The most defensible reading is a swarm of biting flies — common in Egypt, especially near the Nile Delta. Khepri (the scarab beetle god) and other insect-associated Egyptian deities are implicitly challenged.
22: 'But on that day I will set apart (hifleiti) the land of Goshen, where my people dwell, so that no swarms of flies shall be there, that you may know that I am YHWH in the midst of the earth (b'kerev ha'aretz):' The Goshen distinction — the theological watershed of the plague cycle. The selective plague (affecting Egypt but not Goshen) is irrefutable evidence of divine discrimination, ruling out natural explanations. Goshen was located in the eastern Nile Delta region (probably the Wadi Tumilat area); the geographical separation from the main Egyptian population centers makes the distinction spatially verifiable. B'kerev ha'aretz = in the midst of the earth — YHWH's authority is not merely over Israel but over the center of the known world (Egypt).
23: 'I will put a division (pedut) between my people and your people. Tomorrow this sign shall happen.' Pedut = ransom/separation/distinction (from padah = to redeem/ransom; the exodus redemption vocabulary infiltrating the plague narrative). The 'division' is not just geographical but redemptive — the separation of Israel from Egypt at the plagues is the same separation as the redemption from slavery. The plague-distinction anticipates the exodus-distinction.
24: And YHWH did so. There came great swarms of flies into the house of Pharaoh and into his servants' houses. Throughout all the land of Egypt the land was ruined (tishachet) because of the swarms of flies: Shachat = to ruin/corrupt/destroy (the same root as the 'corruption' of the earth before the Flood in Genesis 6:11-12, and the 'destroyer' (mashchit) who struck Egypt's firstborns in Exodus 12:23). The 'ruination' of the land by flies anticipates the final plague's destroyer; the plague cycle is escalating toward the ultimate judgment.
25: Then Pharaoh called Moses and Aaron and said, 'Go, sacrifice to your God within the land.' Pharaoh's first counter-offer: sacrifice in Egypt (not 'in the wilderness' as Moses demanded). This is a significant concession — Pharaoh is acknowledging that his people should be allowed to perform YHWH's worship — but a limited one: the worship must happen within Egypt, under Pharaoh's jurisdiction, not in the freedom of the wilderness. The geography of worship matters; worship 'within the land' means worship still under Egyptian political control.
26: But Moses said, 'It would not be right to do so, for the offerings we shall sacrifice to YHWH our God are an abomination (toevah) to the Egyptians.' Toevah = abomination (the word for ritual taboo in Egypt as well as Israel). Egyptian religious practice had strict taboos about animal sacrifice; certain animals sacred to Egyptian deities (cattle, rams, ibises, crocodiles, cats) could not be slaughtered without risking Egyptian violence. Cows were sacred to Hathor; rams to Amun and Khnum; sacrificing such animals in public in Egypt would provoke Egyptian outrage. Moses's refusal is theologically and practically astute — unrestricted Israelite sacrifice in Egypt was genuinely impossible.
27: 'We must go three days' journey into the wilderness and sacrifice to YHWH our God as he tells us.' The three-day journey (derekh shloshet yamim bamidbar) has been the consistent request since Exodus 3:18 — not a permanent departure but a pilgrimage journey. Three days was a standard travel distance in ancient Near Eastern contexts for a round-trip journey to a sacred site. Whether this was a provisional demand (testing Pharaoh) or the initial phase of a full departure is debated; theologically it establishes the principle that Israelite worship must be geographically outside Egypt's jurisdiction.
28: So Pharaoh said, 'I will let you go to sacrifice to YHWH your God in the wilderness; only you must not go very far away. Plead for me.' Pharaoh's second counter-offer: permission to go, but 'not very far away' (raq haracheiq lo tarbbu lalechet). He is trying to maintain practical control over the departing Israelites by restricting their range. The 'plead for me' (ve'hatiru ba'adee) repeats his earlier prayer-request — Pharaoh is becoming increasingly dependent on Moses's intercession with YHWH even while refusing to fully comply.
29: Moses said, 'Behold, I am going out from you and I will plead with YHWH that the swarms of flies may depart from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people, tomorrow. Only let not Pharaoh cheat again (yosef Pharaoh hateil) by not letting the people go to sacrifice to YHWH.' Hateil = to deceive/deal falsely (from talal = to mock/deceive); the diplomatic bluntness — Moses directly charges Pharaoh with deceptive intention before the next plague has even started. The pattern of promised compliance followed by re-hardening has been consistent enough that Moses's warning is based on track record.
30: So Moses went out from Pharaoh and prayed to YHWH: The prayer is again unstated in content — the narrative moves directly from Moses's departure to YHWH's action. The emphasis is not on Moses's prayer-words but on the relationship: Moses prays, YHWH responds. The intimacy of the Moses-YHWH relationship throughout the plague narrative is contrasted with the distant and coercive relationship between Pharaoh and his gods.
31: And YHWH did as Moses asked, and removed the swarms of flies from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people; not one remained: The total removal ('not one remained' — lo nish'ar echad) is the same formula used for the total destruction of Egypt's firstborns and charioteers. Each act of divine precision — whether removal of plague or execution of judgment — demonstrates the same total control. The complete removal proves YHWH brought the flies and YHWH removed them.
32: But Pharaoh hardened his heart this time also, and did not let the people go: The seventh hardening. Pharaoh's cycle of distress-promise-relief-hardening is now well established. Egyptian royal ideology would have made capitulation to a foreign deity's demands extremely costly politically — every concession to YHWH was a public acknowledgment of Egyptian divine failure. The political stakes of Pharaoh's religious stubbornness are not separable from the theological ones.
